planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown trees in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Shinseiki is one of the most widely grown Asian pears, producing medium-large yellow fruits that are juicy, crisp, and mild-flavored, excellent for Gulf Coast and mild-climate orchards where low-chill Asian pears perform better than European pears.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; requires a pollinator — plant with Nijisseiki, Hosui, or another compatible Asian pear variety for good fruit set. Shinseiki (Japanese for 'New Century') is one of the most important commercial and home-garden Asian pear cultivars worldwide, bred in Japan in 1945 and widely grown in California, Oregon, and southern states; it produces medium-large (6–10 oz), round, smooth-skinned, pale yellow to cream-yellow fruits in August with crisp, white, juicy flesh and mild, refreshing, lightly sweet flavor. Low-chill requirement (approximately 300 chill hours) makes it excellent for Gulf Coast, Texas, and California gardens where traditional European pears fail. Fruits store well for 2–3 months refrigerated; highly productive and relatively disease-resistant compared to European pears. Cross-pollinate with Nijisseiki, Hosui, or another Asian pear for best yields.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Hosui is widely considered the finest-flavored of all commonly available Asian pears — the round, golden-brown, russet-skinned fruits are enormously juicy, sweet, and aromatic in a way that makes them far superior for fresh eating to most other Asian pear varieties.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; requires a pollinator — plant with Shinseiki, Nijisseiki, or Bartlett pear for best fruit set. Hosui (Japanese for 'Abundant Juice') is widely regarded by Asian pear enthusiasts and orchardists as the finest-flavored Asian pear for fresh eating; it produces large (10–14 oz), round, golden-brown russet-skinned fruits with extraordinarily juicy, sweet, highly aromatic flesh that exceeds most other Asian pear varieties in flavor complexity and richness. Ripening in late August, the fruits have a distinctive honey-sweet, floral aroma and tender-crisp texture; they do not store as well as Shinseiki or Nijisseiki (2–3 months maximum refrigerated) but the fresh-off-the-tree quality is outstanding. Best in low-chill areas (approximately 450 chill hours) and performs well in California, Oregon, and the Gulf Coast. Fire blight susceptibility requires attention.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nijisseiki (20th Century) is the historic benchmark Asian pear variety, discovered in Japan in 1888 and still the standard against which all others are measured — producing large, juicy, round, yellow-skinned fruits that exemplify the classic Asian pear form and flavor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; requires cross-pollination with another compatible Asian pear. Nijisseiki (20th Century, in Japanese literally 'twentieth century') is perhaps the most historically significant Asian pear cultivar, discovered as a chance seedling in Matsudo, Chiba Prefecture, Japan in 1888 by Chuhei Matsudo and eventually named for the approaching new century; for most of the 20th century it was Japan's most widely grown pear and still accounts for significant commercial production. The fruits are large (8–12 oz), perfectly round, smooth-skinned, pale yellow-green, and juicy with a mild, refreshing, lightly sweet flavor and crisp, white flesh. Reasonably low chill requirement (approximately 400–500 hours) makes it viable in California, Gulf Coast, and mild Southern gardens. Ripens in August; stores well for 2–4 months refrigerated.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Harrow Sweet is a Canadian-bred European pear released by Agriculture Canada's Harrow Research Station with outstanding fire blight resistance and excellent sweet, buttery fruit — one of the most disease-resistant European pears available for humid climates.

directions: Plant two different pear varieties for cross-pollination; in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun. Harrow Sweet pear was released in 1964 by Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Harrow Research Station in Ontario specifically for its outstanding fire blight resistance (a major disease problem for European pears in humid eastern climates) combined with excellent fruit quality; the medium-large, short-necked, golden-yellow fruits with some russeting ripen in September and have sweet, juicy, buttery flesh with exceptional flavor and keeping quality — storing for 2–3 months in refrigerated storage. Harrow Sweet was selected to fill the gap between early-ripening Bartlett (poor storage) and late-ripening D'Anjou/Bosc types; it is particularly valuable in humid southeastern climates where fire blight pressure makes most European pears unreliable. Partially self-fruitful but yields better with a pollinator.
sources: Purdue University Extension Fruit Growing Guide

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Moonglow is a USDA-released pear variety with good fire blight resistance, soft, sweet, mild, very early-ripening fruit, and adaptability to Southern climates that makes it one of the most recommended pear varieties for zone 6–8 southern gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Moonglow cross-pollinates well with Seckel, Bartlett, Harrow Sweet, and most other European pear varieties. Moonglow pear was released by the USDA in 1960 from a breeding program targeting soft-fleshed, early-ripening pear varieties for Southern gardens; it produces medium-large, pyriform (classic pear-shaped), pale yellow to greenish-yellow fruits that ripen in late July to August — the earliest of the commonly grown European pears. The flesh is white, very soft, mild, and sweet without the grittiness of some older pear varieties; it is a dessert pear best for eating fresh as it does not hold well after picking (must be ripened off the tree like Bartlett). Good fire blight resistance makes it more reliable than Bartlett in humid southeastern gardens. Partially self-fruitful; cross-pollinate with another European pear for best yields.
sources: Virginia Cooperative Extension Fruit Culture Guide
